tips.text: Create a project before starting a session so HagiCode can manage files, context, and history together.
Keep each project focused on related code and materials so sessions do not read unrelated context.
Use a business or repository name for the project so it is easier to find later.
Record the current goal in the project so later sessions can pick up faster.
Put startup steps, account details, and common commands in the project notes to avoid repeating them.
Remove unrelated files before uploading materials so HagiCode can stay focused on the task.
Select the right project before opening a new session so the task lands in the correct context.
When reviewing project history, start with the conclusion from the most recent completed session.
For larger changes, ask HagiCode to write a proposal before deciding whether to implement it.
Make the proposal scope clear so the session does not keep expanding while it runs.
When you are unsure about the approach, ask HagiCode to list multiple options in the proposal.
Risk notes in a proposal help you decide whether the change is worth continuing.
Breaking a proposal into tasks makes execution progress easier to follow.
Review the proposal before implementation to catch missing details or a wrong direction early.
Give each proposal a short, clear name so it is easier to find later.
Record the result after a proposal is finished so you can revisit why the change was made.
Give each session one clear goal so the result is easier to judge.
Start a session by stating the goal, constraints, and expected output directly.
When continuing an old task, tell HagiCode what has changed recently.
In a long session, ask HagiCode for progress checkpoints along the way.
Before pausing, record the next step so resuming the session takes less time.
After a session ends, read the result summary before deciding whether to keep asking follow-up questions.
Correct the direction as soon as it feels wrong instead of overturning the whole session at the end.
If you know the relevant files, give HagiCode the file paths directly.
When something fails, send HagiCode the full error message instead of only saying it failed.
Before running sessions in parallel, split the goal into parts that do not conflict with each other.
Use clear names for parallel sessions, such as proposal design, frontend implementation, or troubleshooting.
When running sessions in parallel, try not to have them edit the same group of files.
After parallel sessions finish, review the results together before deciding which parts to use.
For important tasks, let one session implement and another session focus on review.
Give each agent a clear role, such as implementation, review, testing, or documentation cleanup.
When multiple agents collaborate, define their responsibility boundaries in advance.
During an agent handoff, preserve key conclusions, unfinished work, and important notes.
A review agent is better suited to finding issues than taking on a large rewrite at the same time.
A research agent can gather background first, then hand the work to an implementation agent.
Ask an agent for a short summary when you need to judge the next step quickly.
Claude is well suited for analyzing long materials, comparing approaches, and organizing complex context.
Before complex design work, ask Claude to check whether the reasoning is complete.
When reading a large set of documents, Claude can help extract the key points and constraints.
Codex is well suited for entering a repository, editing code, and running commands to verify the result.
When you do not know where to change something, ask Codex to search the project structure first.
After Codex makes changes, focus on which files changed and why those changes were made.
When you need to inspect code manually, open the project with code-server.
For small manual adjustments, code-server can be more convenient than switching local environments.
Before running a command in code-server, confirm that the terminal is in the target project.
When adjusting an interface, use code-server to preview the actual result.
OmniRoute can connect different models in one place so switching capabilities is easier.
Use a faster model for simple tasks, then switch to a stronger model for complex ones.
When a model is unavailable, OmniRoute fallback routes can reduce interruptions.
With OmniRoute, balance speed, quality, and cost based on how important the task is.
